name: Riverview park
Description/Comments: "Riverview Amusement Park in Chicago which, at the time, housed the largest downhill roller coaster in the world."

name: The Hyde Park Hotel
Description/Comments: "Vitality is what defined the Hyde Park Hotel. The building had been constructed in several stages for Paul Cornell by Starrett & Fuller. The first section of the hotel dates to 1888; it was a pressed brick and stone building, 80-feet-by-85-feet, rising four stories in height. Always with an eye toward the future, Cornell had the walls built strong enough to accommodate additional stories. "

name: Chicago Stadium
replacement: parking lot for the United Center
Description/Comments: "When the Chicago Stadium was built in 1929, it it was the largest indoor arena in the world. It was the home of the NHL Chicago Blackhawks and the NBA’s Chicago Bulls. It was nicknamed the “Madhouse on Madison”, and a feature during events was the playing of the largest Barton Pipe organ ever built. It also hosted five United States presidential nominating conventions. The stadium was built by Paddy Harmon, a promoter, who sank his entire fortune into the project, only to lose control to the stadium shareholders. After exiting receivership in 1935, the Stadium was owned by the Norris and Wirtz families until its closure in 1994 and demolition in 1995. It was replaced by the United Center."

name: Mecca Flats
replacement: Mies van der Rohe’s Crown Hall
Description/Comments: "Originally constructed as a hotel for the World's Columbian Exposition of 1893, the 96-unit Mecca building became an apartment building after the fair. The building was acquired by Illinois Tech around 1941, and was demolished in 1952 to make way for the construction of S.R. Crown Hall."

name: Gerri’s Palm Tavern
replacement: Gallery Guichard Great Migration Sculpture Garden
Description/Comments: "The city of Chicago filed eminent domain proceedings against the owner. The tavern was a center of Black life and culture for decades. It’s my assertion that this act of wanton destruction is perhaps the greatest cultural loss Chicago has seen in decades, made worse by the fact that the city actively pursued that destruction. Mike M"

name: Prentice Women’s Hospital
replacement: Louis A. Simpson and Kimberly K. Querrey Biomedical Research Center
Description/Comments: The Prentice women's hospital relocated to a new, state-of-the-art facility at 250 East Superior Street. The Chicago Landmarks Commission voted against designating the building as a landmark and Northwestern demolished the old hospital

name: Kraft building
Description/Comments: "The historic Kraft building, long bereft of its name-giving company and many of its historic feattures, is expected to fall to the wrecking ball in the next few weeks to make way for a park and two condominium buildings."

name: The Arcade
Description/Comments: The arcade, which opened in 1882 was intended as one of the social and economic cornerstones of the town.

name: United States Custom House, Court House, and Post Office
Description/Comments: This federal building was poorly planned and poorly built,[1] and by the 1890s was considered dangerous and too small.

name: Chicago Federal Building
Description/Comments: housed the Midwest's federal courts, main post office, and other government bureaus

name: Tacoma Building
Description/Comments: an early skyscraper in Chicago

name: Masonic Temple
Description/Comments: it was the tallest building in the city until the 1920s

name: Olson Park and Waterfall
Description/Comments: During the opening of the park in 1935, the land was symbolically deeded to the Native American, in recognition of the 100th anniversary of the expulsion after the Blackhawk war. The Chicago Tribune named Olson Park as the first of "Chicago's Seven Lost Wonders".[

name: Great Central Station
Description/Comments: "Great Central Station, also known as Great Central Depot,[nb 1] was an intercity train station in Chicago, Illinois, owned by the Illinois Central Railroad (IC). It opened in 1856 and for a time was the largest building in downtown Chicago."

name: Zum Deutschen Eck
Description/Comments: Some blame the son of the original owner, Al Wirth Jr., for taking the money and running, leaving the loyalists without their beloved Zum Deutschen Eck. But, as with anything, life moves on…“Zum Deutschen Eck” was a popular restaurant serving German fare and beer. It was torn down and only a plaque reminds us of this piece of Chicago history today. Angelique B.

name: The Schiller Building
Description/Comments: "Originally funded by prominent figures like Anton C. Hesing and designed by Adler & Sullivan, the 240-foot tower featured an ornate terra cotta  facade with intricate ironwork, characteristic of the Chicago School architectural style. The building initially served as a venue for German-language operas and cultural events. In the late 1890s, after a shift in patronage, it transitioned to hosting a variety of touring stage shows. Its 1,300-seat theater underwent name changes, from the Dearborn Theater in 1898 to the Garrick Theater in 1903, and would host prominent figures to take the stage such Jane Addams, Eugene V. Debs, and Mae West." The demolition instigated a large outcry and is considered to be one of the first wide-scale preservation efforts in Chicago.

name: Lakeside Building
Description/Comments: "The Lakeside building is the outgrowth of a company, formed by a few of our leading citizens for the purpose of carrying on the publishing and printing business on a more extended scale than had hitherto been done in the West. ... The late Levi A. Leiter had disposed of loop property amounting to $4,250,000 in the vicinity of Clark, Adams and Quincy streets"

name: The Lake Shore Drive ‘S’ Curve Bottleneck
Description/Comments: It featured two sharp 90-degree turns, fitting for the slower vehicles of the time. However, as traffic speed increased, this once necessary design gradually became a source of congestion and safety concerns.

name: Louis Henry Sullivan House
Description/Comments: "Known as the “prophet of modern architecture,” Louis Sullivan advocated creating buildings that honestly mirrored their time, place and technology. Damaged by fire and abandoned, the house was demolished in 1970"

name: The 1885 Cook County Courthouse and City Hall
Description/Comments: The inadequacies of the recently opened buildings made it evident that consideration must be given to the need for a new city hall and a new county building.

name: The Historical Haven School
Description/Comments: "The dedicatory exercises of the new Haven School building, drew together a large audience of parents, and friends of education. The occasion was bone of rare interest, and will long be remembered as one of the pleasing as well as important events which mark the progress of Chicago in the great cause of education." "Mr. Strensland said it was about time to consider the matter of tearing down the Haven School and beginning the constrcution of a new one on its site."

name: Marshall Field House
Description/Comments: Built to last 1,000 years, the landmark to the gayety and extravagance of society of the last century is as solid as ever. It was the first house in Chicago to have electric lights.

name: The William Borden House
Description/Comments: The demolition of the house is often noted by architectural historians as a major loss of a Gilded Age masterpiece, though its interior was extensively photographed by Richard Nickel before its destruction.

name: Swift & Company Meatpacking Warehouse
Description/Comments: warehouse. Chicago: Swift's headquarters was a major center for the nation's beef supply

name: John Ramcke House
Description/Comments: "Landmarks Illinois research shows the Queen Anne-style home was built in 1888 for John Ramcke, a leader of the construction trades who apparently moved a few years later. The building in question, at 2028 N. Seminary, carries an “orange” rating under Chicago’s classification of older or historic buildings. It doesn’t protect it but puts a 90-day hold on any demolition order, providing time to get people to talk."

name: Italian Court Building
Description/Comments: The Ida B. Wells Homes, which also included the Clarence Darrow Homes and Madden Park Homes, was a Chicago Housing Authority (CHA) public housing project

name: Chicago Machinery Exchange

name: Ida B. Wells Homes
Description/Comments: was a public housing project

name: W.J. Cassidy Tire Company
replacement: new apartment complex, dubbed "Cassidy on Canal",
Description/Comments: "Originally a factory and warehouse for the Tyler & Hippach Mirror Company, Cassidy was designed by Henry J. Schlacks, who had once worked in the offices of Adler & Sullivan and is best known for churches around Chicagoland. The irony is that just after five years after its construction Cassidy became the largest building ever moved (at the time) to make way for the Chicago and North Western Terminal."

name: Klas
Description/Comments: "Klas was a restaurant in Cicero, Illinois, founded in 1922 and shuttered in 2016, which specialized in Bohemian cuisine.The restaurant served numerous high ranking Czech and American officials, along with many other notable individuals, and served as an important institution for the Czech American community."

name: Mickelberry Sausage Company plant

Description/Comments: The owner having to file bankruptcy and close down the theater in 1968.

name: McCarthy Building
Description/Comments: was demolished in 1989 during the clearing of what is known as Chicago's Block 37

name: Tabernacle Community Hospital and Health Center
Description/Comments: In 1977, the Chicago Board of Health prohibited the hospital from admitting new patients, and then from performing surgery, because of "the hospital's tax troubles, allegations of improper use of federal funds, and a series of fires that destroyed or damaged many of the hospital's administrative and patient records.

name: Paradise Theatre
Description/Comments: Balaban and Katz closed the theater, demolished the building and sold the land to be developed as a supermarket

name: Savoy Ballroom
Description/Comments: Originally featuring primarily Jazz artists, including Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, Duke Ellington, Earl Hines, Stan Kenton, Dizzy Gillespie, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Gene Krupa, Woody Herman

name: Old University of Chicago
Description/Comments: The school was constantly plagued by financial difficulties and was forced to close in 1886.

name: Academy of Music
Description/Comments: The Academy of Music was destroyed by fire on February 5, 1878. Then they rebuilt a second academy. Short lived, the second theatre was destroyed by fire on October 12, 1880. The third academy of music opened in 1882.

name: Michael Reese Hospital
Description/Comments: Portions of the hospital campus were over 100 years old and believed by some preservationists to hold historic value. The older buildings were constructed in an ornate style.

name: Dexter Building
Description/Comments: It was one of the earliest surviving Louis Sullivan buildings, "designated as a Chicago Landmark in 1996 and was described by the Landmarks Division of the City of Chicago Department of Planning and Development as an "irreplaceable link in the chain of work of one of the nation's most important architectural partnerships"."

name: Crawford Station
Description/Comments: It was closed in 2012 after a long battle with the community over pollution.

name: Stateway Gardens
Description/Comments: Finished by the late 1950s, Stateway Gardens was plagued by a large amount of gang violence, organized crime, and drug abuse. The area gradually became more neglected and underserved by city authorities and the Chicago Police Department which led to mass abandonment and urban decay.

name: Montauk Building
Description/Comments: "Designed by John Wellborn Root Sr. and Daniel Burnham, it was built in 1882–1883, and was demolished in 1902. Author Erik Larson claims that the Montauk became the first building to be called a "skyscraper" ("

name: Midway Gardens
Description/Comments: Midway Gardens and declared bankruptcy in March 1916

name: Home Insurance Building
Description/Comments: Destroyed to pave path for the Field Building widely considered the first skyscraper

name: Brach's Candy Factory
Description/Comments: was once one of the country's largest confectionary plants

name: Belmont Rocks

name: Chicago Stock Exchange
Description/Comments: "This building was considered a masterpiece of commercial architecture and was designed by Adler & Sullivan."

name: Jane Addams Homes

name: Dexter Park
Description/Comments: "The horse racing track was also the first home of the White Stockings baseball team and hosted other events like wrestling."

name: International Amphitheatre
Description/Comments: "This was known as the site of four Democratic National Conventions and two Republican National Conventions. It also served as an entertainment venue that hosted Elvis, the Beatles, and the Bulls."

name: Hull House
Description/Comments: "Hull House became a leading model for social reform in the United States. It introduced services like childcare, public playgrounds, and educational classes for immigrant communities. They were also active in the reforms of child labor laws, women's rights, and education."

name: Truc Lam Buddhist Temple
Description/Comments: "Became a case of failed landmark protection and the demolition also served as a sign of gentrification in the neighborhood."

name: Fort Dearborn
Description/Comments: "A rebuilding of the original U.S. Army fort that was burned down during the 1812 Battle of Fort Dearborn. Most of the structure was destroyed in a fire or torn down to make way for the city's expansion."

name: John and Mary Jones House
Description/Comments: "This was once the a stop of the Underground Railroad station and was home to prominent Black abolitionists."

name: Central Music Hall
Description/Comments: "Widely considered as Adler's first important building, and served as a vital cultural engine in the Loop, bridging commerce with the arts."

name: Chicago Central Station
Description/Comments: "The station was a gateway to Chicago for African Americans who settled in nearby neighborhoods such as Bronzeville."

name: Archer Daniels Midland Wheat Mill
replacement: In development for a mixed-use project
Description/Comments: "Largest flour mill in the state when it was first developed."

name: Chicago and Northwestern Passenger Terminal
Description/Comments: "This terminal was known for not only its distinctive clock tower, but also for setting new standards for passenger care."

name: Comiskey Park

name: Chicago Grand Central Station

name: The Morrison Hotel
Description/Comments: "This was once the world's tallest hotel for 30 years and remained the tallest hotel in Chicago up until it was demolished."

name: Sauganash Hotel

name: Wigwam

name: Former Chicago Sun Times Building
Description/Comments: "Known to be an eyesore and served as the headquarters to Chicago Sun Times Newspaper."

name: Henrici's
Description/Comments: "This location was actually the second after the original was destroyed in the Great Fire. The restaurant was known as "Chicago's Most Famous Restaurant."

name: Granada Theatre
Description/Comments: "This was once a popular rock-concert venue and movie theatre. At the time, it was one of the three largest theatres in the city."

name: A.J. Stone Building

name: Iriquois Theatre

name: Robert Taylor Homes
Description/Comments: "This was the largest public housing project in United States history prior to its demolition."

name: SMC Cartage Co. garage
Description/Comments: "This was the site of the infamous St. Valentine’s Day Massacre."

name: Cabrini-Green Homes
Description/Comments: "Considered a national symbol of the failure of high-rise public housing policies."

name: Palmer Mansion
Description/Comments: "At one point, this was the largest private residence in the city. The construction of the mansion essentially led to the establishment of the Gold Coast neighborhood.

name: White City Amusement Park
Description/Comments: "A premier Chicago entertainment hub that was promoted as the largest of its kind in the US. It also hosted the first Goodyear Blimp assembly."

name: Garrick Theater
Description/Comments: "This theater had one of the most publicized preservation campaigns in the city's history."

name: Edgewater Beach Hotel
Description/Comments: "The hotel was known for its notable guests, including Martin Luther King Jr.."

name: Mode Theatre
Description/Comments: "This was a notable neighborhood cinema."

name: Grand Pacific Hotel
Description/Comments: "One of two prominent hotels built in Chicago after the Great Chicago Fire."

name: Union Stock Yards

name: Vendome Theater

name: The Pullman Building
Description/Comments: "This was the headquarter of George Pullman's railroad car empire."

name: Pilgrim Baptist Church
replacement: Planned: National Museum of Gospel Music

name: Chicago Coliseum
Description/Comments: "This was a major political and cultural hub that also served as a rock concert venue, an arena for hosting the first-ever roller derby event and early interracial basketball tournaments, as well as ice hockey."

name: Pekin Theatre

name: Grand Avenue Radar Track
Description/Comments: "The site was used for testing mobile radar units during World War II."

name: Damen Silos

name: Little Jim’s Tavern
Description/Comments: "This was Boystown's first-established and longest-running gay bar."

name: Man's Country/Chicago
Description/Comments: "Chicago's longest-running gay bathhouse that hosted celebrity performances and served as a concert venue, dance club, community space, gym, and sex club."

name: Hyde Park House
Description/Comments: "Notable hotel that Mary Todd Lincoln and the Prince of Wales stayed at during their time in Chicago."

name: Maxwell Street Market
Description/Comments: "A crucial cultural hub for immigrant and Black communities, and is considered the birthplace of Chicago Blues. It was at one point considered the world’s largest open-air market"

name: Mahlon D. Ogden House
Description/Comments: "This was known for being the only house in the path of the Great Fire of 1871 which was not burned."

name: Temperance Temple
Description/Comments: "A significant intersection of female activism, philanthropy, and corporate ambition in late 19th-century America happened in this building."

name: The Relic House
Description/Comments: "This was a symbol of the city's resilience and served as a popular tourist attraction. It eventually transitioned into a saloon, German restaurant, and even a speakeasy during the Prohibition era. It was also a known gathering spot for artists."

name: Rainbo Arena
Description/Comments: "Multipurpose building with a long history of occupancies: outdoor gardens that was transformed into an indoor sports arena, having a training rink for several Olympic figure skaters that was also used as the Blackhawk's practice facility, and even housing a pro bowling alley. Afterwards, when it became the Kinetic Playground up until its demolition, the location was known as the driving force in the music business for hosting famous rock bands and musicians."

name: McCormick Reaper Works II
replacement: Various manufacturing, commercial, and industrial facilities
Description/Comments: "At its peak, the complex produced the famous McCormick reaper, which revolutionized farming, along with other agricultural machinery and even military equipment."
